comments.text: 1. This problem also appears in the following volume, which seems to be the original source: P. Erdős, Problem sessions, In: Ordered Sets (Proc. NATO Adv. Study), edited by I. Rival, Dordrecht: Reidel (1981), 860--861. 2. In [Gu83], Guy writes: "[$\ldots$] We have no satisfactory estimate of $n_0(r)$. The content of the previous two paragraphs will be a small subset of a forthcoming paper of Erdős, Szemerédi and Trotter." However, I have not been able to locate any related publication in MathSciNet, Google Scholar, the Erdős paper website, or on Trotter's personal homepage. 3. Let $n_0(r)$ be the threshold such that whenever $n>n_0(r)$ one can achieve $n-3$ distinct set sizes in such a family. My friend He and I have just written a paper (arXiv:2602.09803v1). By iterating ChatGPT-5.2 Thinking hundreds of times, we made some progress on this problem: $n_0(2)=3$ and $n_0(3)=8$, and moreover for every integer $r\ge 4$ one has\[ 2r+2 \ \le\ n_0(r)\ \le\ 2r+2\log_2 r + O(\log_2\log_2 r). \] (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
nice! presumably for each $k\ge 3$, there exists some $C_k$ so that $g(n,(C_k-o(1))n)\ge n-k$ but $g(n,(C_k+o(1))n)<n-k$. do you have a guess for this?

Thank you, Zach. This is a very good question, which generalizes the original Erdős--Trotter problem. For integers $\delta\ge 3$ and $r\ge 2$, we define\[ n_{0}^{(\delta)}(r):=\min\Bigl\{n_0:\ \forall\,n>n_0,\ g(n,r)\ge n-\delta\Bigr\}. \]Then one can obtain the following conclusions (proofs can be found in this note): 1. If $\delta\ge 5$, then \(n_{0}^{(\delta)}(r)=O(\sqrt r)=o(r)\). In particular, the constant $C_{\delta}$ in your conjecture is $+\infty$. 2. When $\delta=4$, one can get a partial result: \(n_{0}^{(4)}(r) \ge \lceil \frac{r + 3}{3} \rceil\) for $r\geq 12$. Therefore, the constant $C_{4}$ in your conjecture lies in the interval $[1/2,\,3]$. EDIT: I don't have a good guess for the specific value of $C_4$ at the moment, but I'm happy to hear your thoughts and discuss it further.

This shows that $n_0(r)\sim 2r$ as $r \to \infty$, so we know the first order behaviour of $n_0(r)$. Since the problem was phrased quite loosely, I'm minded to mark this as solved, but would like to hear what you (and others) think: do you think that the really interesting behaviour lies in the finer behaviour of $n_0(r)-2r$, or do you agree that this problem as Erdős likely originally intended it has been solved in spirit?

Numerical experiments show that for $4 \leq r \leq 10$, we have $n_0(r) \leq 2r + 4$. Thus, I am not sure whether it is possible that for sufficiently large $r$, there exists a constant $C \in \{2, 3, 4\}$ such that $n_0(r) = 2r + C$. I have not yet found a construction that yields $n_0(r) = 2r + O(1)$, but such a construction might exist.
